tossups.question: s: “If he negs again tell him not to keep buzzing for I have left.” EXTRAS This author attacked critics in “Epistle to the Reader,” which prefaces a play about Haunce van Ezel, the title “Dutch lover.” John Phillip Kemble’s production Love in Many Masks adapts a play by this author based on Thomas Killigrew’s Thomaso. Dr. Baliardo marries his daughters to the Prince of Thunderland and the Emperor of the (*) Moon in another play by this author. Florinda marries Colonel Belville in a play by this author subtitled “The Banish’d Cavaliers,” which sees Angellica Bianca hold Hellena’s lover Willmore at gunpoint during Carnival. For 10 points, name this female Restoration playwright of The Rover.
tossups.question_sanitized: s: "If he negs again tell him not to keep buzzing for I have left." EXTRAS This author attacked critics in "Epistle to the Reader," which prefaces a play about Haunce van Ezel, the title "Dutch lover." John Phillip Kemble's production Love in Many Masks adapts a play by this author based on Thomas Killigrew's Thomaso. Dr. Baliardo marries his daughters to the Prince of Thunderland and the Emperor of the (*) Moon in another play by this author. Florinda marries Colonel Belville in a play by this author subtitled "The Banish'd Cavaliers," which sees Angellica Bianca hold Hellena's lover Willmore at gunpoint during Carnival. For 10 points, name this female Restoration playwright of The Rover.
tossups.answer: Aphra Behn (the second line refers to The Rover)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Aphra Behn (the second line refers to The Rover)

tossups.question: In the dedication of one work, this author states that “my Epilogue was promis’d me by a Person who had surely made it good”, possibly referring to another author affected by venereal disease. Another work by this author features a chimney-sweep who pretends to be a nobleman, named Guiliom. A character in a play by this author states “if we part so, let me die like a Bird upon a Bough”, and concludes by speaking of “the Storms o’th’ Marriage-Bed”. In one of this author’s plays, Euphemia marries Alonzo instead of Haunce van Ezel, the titular lover from a work released during the (*) Third Anglo-Dutch War, while another of their plays based on Thomas Killigrew’s Thomaso features the characters Hellena and Willmore in Naples at Carnival. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover, a female playwright of the Restoration.
tossups.question_sanitized: In the dedication of one work, this author states that "my Epilogue was promis'd me by a Person who had surely made it good", possibly referring to another author affected by venereal disease. Another work by this author features a chimney-sweep who pretends to be a nobleman, named Guiliom. A character in a play by this author states "if we part so, let me die like a Bird upon a Bough", and concludes by speaking of "the Storms o'th' Marriage-Bed". In one of this author's plays, Euphemia marries Alonzo instead of Haunce van Ezel, the titular lover from a work released during the (*) Third Anglo-Dutch War, while another of their plays based on Thomas Killigrew's Thomaso features the characters Hellena and Willmore in Naples at Carnival. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover, a female playwright of the Restoration.
tossups.answer: Aphra Behn (The first line is a possible reference to Edward Ravenscroft in the dedication of The Dutch Lover; the second line refers to The False Count.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Aphra Behn (The first line is a possible reference to Edward Ravenscroft in the dedication of The Dutch Lover; the second line refers to The False Count.)

tossups.question: The epilogue of one play by this author is spoken by John Freeman’s secret lover, Lady Desbro. That play by this author ends with Lord Lambert being imprisoned after the King’s forces take London. Dr. Baliardo attempts to marry his daughter and niece to aliens in one play by this author of The Roundheads. Angellica Bianca calls off the murder of the (*) title character of this author’s most famous play, Willmore, who later exchanges vows of love with Hellena. The title character of a novel by this author of The Emperor of the Moon is disemboweled and dismembered after killing his lover Imoinda and leading a failed slave revolt in Suriname. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover and Oroonoko, the premier female playwright of the Restoration.

The title character of a novel by this author of The Emperor of the Moon is disemboweled and dismembered after killing his lover Imoinda and leading a failed slave revolt in Suriname. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover and Oroonoko, the premier female playwright of the Restoration.

tossups.question: A play by this author ends with dancing characters emerging from a giant zodiac. Lucetta seduces a man but drops him down a trapdoor once he has undressed in a play by this author, in which a man tells a nunnery-bound woman that “there’s no sinner like a young saint.” Men disguise themselves as aliens to convince Dr. Baliardo to allow their marriages in a play by this author. Virginia Woolf wrote that “all women… ought to let flowers fall upon” the tomb of this author, who described the romance of Hellena and Willmore in a play subtitled The Banish’d Cavaliers. This author of The Emperor of the Moon may have gathered material for a novel about a slave revolt while serving as a spy in Surinam. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover and Oronooko, a female Restoration dramatist.

tossups.question: In a play by this author, a character urges, “Hang the malicious world––There’s charms in wealth and honour, but none half so powerful as love” to persuade her sister Marcella to escape a “dull virtuous life” with Octavio by pretending to be a prostitute. Mr. Jevern states that the “industrious Stage / Has strove to please a dull ungrateful Age” in the prologue of another play by this author, in which Elaria and Bellemante are forbidden to marry human suitors by their father, Doctor Baliardo, who wants to marry his daughters to (*) alien suitors instead. This author of The Feign’d Curtizans wrote a play in which the vengeful Angellica Bianca falls in love with and later attempts to murder Willmore. In A Room of One’s Own, Virginia Woolf urged women to drop flowers on the grave of this playwright of The Emperor of the Moon. For 10 points, name this Restoration author of The Rover and Oroonoko.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a play by this author, a character urges, "Hang the malicious world--There's charms in wealth and honour, but none half so powerful as love" to persuade her sister Marcella to escape a "dull virtuous life" with Octavio by pretending to be a prostitute. Mr. Jevern states that the "industrious Stage / Has strove to please a dull ungrateful Age" in the prologue of another play by this author, in which Elaria and Bellemante are forbidden to marry human suitors by their father, Doctor Baliardo, who wants to marry his daughters to (*) alien suitors instead. This author of The Feign'd Curtizans wrote a play in which the vengeful Angellica Bianca falls in love with and later attempts to murder Willmore. In A Room of One's Own, Virginia Woolf urged women to drop flowers on the grave of this playwright of The Emperor of the Moon. For 10 points, name this Restoration author of The Rover and Oroonoko.

tossups.question: In one poem by this author “Th' Insensible fell weeping in his Hands” after Lysander cannot sexually satisfy Cloris in the poem “The Disappointment”. This writer created Wilmore, an Englishman in Naples who falls in love with Hellena, in a revision of a Thomas Kilgrew play. In a novel by this author, (*) Imoinda is reunited with her Coromantin lover in Suriname, but he cuts her head off after organizing a slave revolt. This author’s first novel The Fair Jilt is said to be first English novel by a woman. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover, a female Restoration playwright who created Prince Oroonoko.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one poem by this author "Th' Insensible fell weeping in his Hands" after Lysander cannot sexually satisfy Cloris in the poem "The Disappointment". This writer created Wilmore, an Englishman in Naples who falls in love with Hellena, in a revision of a Thomas Kilgrew play. In a novel by this author, (*) Imoinda is reunited with her Coromantin lover in Suriname, but he cuts her head off after organizing a slave revolt. This author's first novel The Fair Jilt is said to be first English novel by a woman. For 10 points, name this author of The Rover, a female Restoration playwright who created Prince Oroonoko.

tossups.question: In a comedy by this author, the daughter and niece of Dr. Balliardo employ a harlequin and a valet to convince him that their lovers are alien visitors. Cloris is distressed to find “beneath the verdant leaves a snake” which prompts her to flee “like lightning through the grove” in a poem by this author in which Lisander curses “his birth, his fate, his stars” after finding himself unable to achieve erection. This author of “The Emperor of the Moon” wrote a play in which the revenge-seeking courtesan Angellica Bianca disrupts the budding romance between Hellena and Captain Willmore. This author of “The Disappointment” also created a character from Coromantien who kills his lover Imoinda after being enslaved and taken to Suriname. Women are urged to drop flowers on this author's tomb because “it was she who earned them the right to speak their minds” in Virginia Woolf’s “A Room of One’s Own.” For 10 points, name this English spy who wrote The Rover and Oroonoko.

For 10 points, name this English spy who wrote The Rover and Oroonoko.

tossups.question: This author described an unfulfilling liaison between Cloris and Lysander in one poem. A play by this author depicts a girl who fends off a rapist by showing him a diamond ring, thereby indicating that she is a “person of quality.” That girl’s sister, Hellena, is seduced by a man who notes, “There’s no sinner like a young saint.” In her best-known novel, this author created characters who are given the Christian names (*) Caesar and Clemene. At the end of that novel, the title character leads a slave revolt against deputy governor Byam in Surinam and mourns his decapitated lover Imoinda. For 10 points, identify this Restoration playwright and spy who wrote The Rover and Oroonoko.

tossups.question: One work by this author in written as a manual in which Iris suggests engaging in forced entertainment and writing twice every day to Damon during their time apart. This author described a character who is “as much unused to fear, / As he was capable of love” in a poem in which that man, Lysander, finishes too quickly for Cloris. This author of The Lover’s Watch wrote an epistolary novel about the related lovers Silvia and Philander. The prostitute Angellica Bianca opts not to order the shooting of (*) Willmore at the end of one of her plays. The poem “The Disappointment” was written by this woman who described Imoinda’s death at the hand of her beloved during a slave rebellion in Suriname. For 10 points, name this British female restoration author and spy who wrote The Rover and Oroonoko.

bonuses.leadin: A character in this play convinces his love interest to judge the clothes of everyone on the Mall, and be “malicious on all the ill-fashioned things we meet.” For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A character in this play convinces his love interest to judge the clothes of everyone on the Mall, and be "malicious on all the ill-fashioned things we meet." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play in which Lady Woodvill reluctantly consents to a match between her daughter Harriet and the rake Dorimant.
Overly-fashionable fops like Sir Fopling Flutter, who titles The Man of Mode, were popular comic characters in drama from this historical period, which is named for Charles II’s return to the throne.
A “fake” marriage ceremony turns out to have been legal all along in this author’s play The Town Fop. “The Banish'd Cavaliers” is the subtitle of a play this author set during the Carnival in Naples.
parts_sanitized: Name this play in which Lady Woodvill reluctantly consents to a match between her daughter Harriet and the rake Dorimant.
Overly-fashionable fops like Sir Fopling Flutter, who titles The Man of Mode, were popular comic characters in drama from this historical period, which is named for Charles II's return to the throne.
A "fake" marriage ceremony turns out to have been legal all along in this author's play The Town Fop. "The Banish'd Cavaliers" is the subtitle of a play this author set during the Carnival in Naples.
answers_sanitized: The Man of Mode (by George Etherege)
Restoration era [or English Restoration; or Stuart Restoration]
Aphra Behn (The play is The Rover.)

bonuses.leadin: In The Country Wife, a work in this genre, the rake Horner pretends to be impotent in order to be given permission to socialize with married wives. For 10 points each:

parts: Name this genre of theatre whose namesake “Restoration” type flourished during the reign of Charles II. Aristophanes is considered the “father of” this genre.
This leading author of Restoration comedies wrote a play set during Carnival in which Hellena dresses as a man in an attempt to seduce Willmore.
Before becoming a playwright, Aphra Behn worked in this profession for Charles II. John le Carré wrote about George Smiley and Alec Leamas, who both have this profession.

bonuses.leadin: The play Lust’s Dominion, now attributed to Thomas Dekker, may have been based on a tragedy named for one of these people. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The play Lust's Dominion, now attributed to Thomas Dekker, may have been based on a tragedy named for one of these people. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this kind of person who subtitles a tragedy adapted from Lust’s Dominion, in which the title character seduces a queen, convinces her to poison her husband, Philip, has the queen killed, and then pursues her daughter, Leonora.
Those events occur in Abdelazer, the only tragedy by this author, who wrote a 1677 comedy about English tourists in Naples during Carnival.
A more famous Moorish protagonist in Renaissance tragedy is this Shakespeare character, who is tricked by Iago into killing Desdemona.

answers: Moor [accept The Spanish Moor’s Tragedy; accept Abdelazer; or, the Moor’s Revenge; prompt on Abdelazer by asking “What kind of person is that play subtitled for?”]

bonuses.leadin: Henry Purcell wrote a set of incidental music to this playwright’s Abdelazer, whose second movement later supplied the theme to Britten’s Young Person’s Guide to the Orchestra. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Henry Purcell wrote a set of incidental music to this playwright's Abdelazer, whose second movement later supplied the theme to Britten's Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of an early novel whose adaptation by Thomas Southerne was partially set by Purcell. This author wrote about the courtesan Angellica in The Rover.

The same year he composed Abdelazer, Purcell also wrote incidental music for Bonduca, one of the many collaborations between Francis Beaumont and this playwright. This playwright also collaborated with Shakespeare on Two Noble Kinsmen.

Purcell also set plays by Fletcher and Southerne about these people. Queen Elizabeth’s request for Falstaff to fall in love resulted in a Shakespeare play titled for “Merry” examples of these people “of Windsor.”
parts_sanitized: Name this author of an early novel whose adaptation by Thomas Southerne was partially set by Purcell. This author wrote about the courtesan Angellica in The Rover.

The same year he composed Abdelazer, Purcell also wrote incidental music for Bonduca, one of the many collaborations between Francis Beaumont and this playwright. This playwright also collaborated with Shakespeare on Two Noble Kinsmen.

Purcell also set plays by Fletcher and Southerne about these people. Queen Elizabeth's request for Falstaff to fall in love resulted in a Shakespeare play titled for "Merry" examples of these people "of Windsor."
answers: wives [accept Rule a Wife, Have a Wife or The Wives Excuse or Merry Wives of Windsor]
answers_sanitized: wives [accept Rule a Wife, Have a Wife or The Wives Excuse or Merry Wives of Windsor]

bonuses.leadin: In this play, Lucetta tricks Blunt by opening a trapdoor under the bed he’s laying on, allowing her to take his money and clothes. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In this play, Lucetta tricks Blunt by opening a trapdoor under the bed he's laying on, allowing her to take his money and clothes. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play in which two sisters go disguised during Carnival in Naples while the licentious Captain Willmore toys with the courtesan Angellica Bianca.
Colonel Fainwell pretends to be invisible by hiding under a trapdoor to trick Periwinkle, one of Anne Lovely’s four guardians, in this author’s 1718 play A Bold Stroke for a Wife.
A satirical caricature of Thomas Shadwell falls through a trapdoor at the end of Mac Flecknoe, a poem by this first Poet Laureate of England.

Susanna Centlivre [or Susanna Freeman; or Susanna Carroll]

bonuses.leadin: Charles Gildon was a rakish writer of often wholly invented biographies and, as such, lived a varied literary life. For 10 points each:
parts: Gildon began his career with a pseudo-history of John Dunton’s Athenian Society, which invented this type of work. Pauline Phillips wrote the far more modern Dear Abby work of this type.

To criticize plot holes, Gildon wrote a pamphlet which ends with this character’s creator eating his own big books. A series of non-fiction essays in the voice of this character comprises the book Serious Reflections.

Gildon invented his biography of this author, arguing that her stories were just recollections of her life. He suggested that this author had an affair with her most famous novel’s protagonist, a prince kidnapped from Coramantien.

bonuses.leadin: As a child, this author likely spent time in the colony of Suriname, inspiring a novel about a prince of Coramantien who falls in love with Imoinda before he is enslaved and brought to the New World. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this playwright, poet, and author of the novel Oroonoko.
Like John Dryden, Behn wrote during this era of British literature and history, so named because Charles II regained the throne in 1660.

Behn used this goddess's name as her code name during a largely unsuccessful stint as a spy in Antwerp. With "Redux," this Greek goddess and daughter of Eos titles a John Dryden poem praising the "happy prince" and "happy age" of Charles II.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the reception of Aesop’s fables in English literature, for 10 points each.
parts: This author wrote a rhyming quatrain version of “The Fox and the Grapes” for Francis Barlow’s popular 1687 illustrated edition. This Restoration-era playwright wrote The Emperor of the Moon and the prose work Oroonoko.

This woman sums up her view that history would have been different “if women had written stories” with the question “who painted the lion?” from Aesop’s fable “The Man and the Lion.” This wife from The Canterbury Tales tells a story about “what women want most.”

Two exchanges between Aesop and his lover Rhodopè (“rod-OH-pee”) appear in a series of dialogues by this Romantic poet. This poet memorialized a lord’s daughter in a poem that opens “Ah, what avails the sceptred race.”
answers: Walter Savage Landor (The collection is Imaginary Conversations, and the poem is “Rose Aylmer.”)

packet.name: J – Berkeley B, Bristol A, Columbia B, Florida B, Imperial A, Mississippi State, Northwestern, Texas A&M A

answers_sanitized: Walter Savage Landor (The collection is Imaginary Conversations, and the poem is "Rose Aylmer.")

bonuses.leadin: A poem by this author in which an “o’er-ravished shepherd” is “unable to perform the sacrifice” was originally misattributed to the Earl of Rochester. For 10 points each:
parts: The impotent Lysander appears in “The Disappointment,” a poem by what 17th-century member of the “fair triumvirate of wit”?
The maid in “The Disappointment” has this pastoral name, which Behn used in several poems. A “fair” woman with this name masturbates while lying in a pigsty in a poem by the Earl of Rochester.
In A Room of One’s Own, Virginia Woolf writes that women should let flowers fall on Behn’s tomb in this abbey. Behn is buried in this abbey’s East Cloister, rather than in its Poets’ Corner.
answers: Aphra Behn [prompt on Aphra Johnson] (The “fair triumvirate of wit” also included Eliza Haywood and Delarivier Manley.)
Westminster Abbey [or Collegiate Church of Saint Peter at Westminster]
answers_sanitized: Aphra Behn [prompt on Aphra Johnson] (The "fair triumvirate of wit" also included Eliza Haywood and Delarivier Manley.)
Cloris [accept "Fair Chloris in a pigsty lay"]
Westminster Abbey [or Collegiate Church of Saint Peter at Westminster]

bonuses.leadin: A play by this author explores the sexual adventures of Florinda, Hellena, and Angellica Bianca during the Naples carnival. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of The Rover. The protagonist is reunited with Imoinda (im-oh-IN-dah) in this author's novella Oroonoko, often considered the earliest British novel.
Behn's other frank depictions of female sexuality include a prose work titled for some of these texts between "a Nobleman and his Sister." Epistolary novels are comprised of these texts.

Lysander throws himself on the maid Cloris in this Behn poem, originally attributed to the Earl of Rochester. Lysander finds himself in "the Hell of Impotence" after he fails to get an erection in this poem.
answers: love letters [or Love Letters between a Nobleman and his Sister]
answers_sanitized: love letters [or Love Letters between a Nobleman and his Sister]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A play by this author explores the sexual adventures of Florinda, Hellena, and Angellica Bianca during the Naples carnival. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this author of The Rover. The protagonist is reunited with Imoinda (im-oh-IN-dah) in this author's novella Oroonoko, often considered the earliest British novel.
Behn's other frank depictions of female sexuality include a prose work titled for some of these texts between "a Nobleman and his Sister." Epistolary novels are comprised of these texts.

Lysander throws himself on the maid Cloris in this Behn poem, originally attributed to the Earl of Rochester. Lysander finds himself in "the Hell of Impotence" after he fails to get an erection in this poem.

bonuses.leadin: In the early part of this century, women readers and writers dominated the prose genre of Amatory Fiction. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this century, during which Eliza Haywood and Delarivier Manley became literary celebrities. Ian Watt’s The Rise of the Novel analyzes three writers from this century, including the author of Moll Flanders.

Haywood and Manley made up the “fair triumvirate of wit” along with this author of The Fair Jilt. One of this author’s characters throws a bit of his own throat at a man who finds him over the body of Imoinda.
Eliza Haywood so dominated the novel market that this writer satirized her as “Mrs. Novel” in The Author’s Farce. This rival of Samuel Richardson is better known for Shamela and Tom Jones.
answers_sanitized: Aphra Behn (The second clue refers to Oroonoko.)

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about humor derived from male impotence in Restoration writings, for 10 points each.
parts: This author mocked bad writers as those who “Rhyme with all the Rage of Impotence” in his Essay on Criticism. This British satirist also wrote The Dunciad.

Lady Fidget’s husband and Mrs. Squeamish’s grandmother listen to Horner deliver double entendres about his “china collection” while pretending to be impotent in this Restoration comedy by William Wycherley.

The shepherd Lysander falls “weeping in his Hands” due to impotence in this poet’s “The Disappointment.” Though first credited to the Earl of Rochester, this poet republished it alongside homoerotic poems about Amintas in “Poems upon Several Occasions.”
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about humor derived from male impotence in Restoration writings, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: This author mocked bad writers as those who "Rhyme with all the Rage of Impotence" in his Essay on Criticism. This British satirist also wrote The Dunciad.

The shepherd Lysander falls "weeping in his Hands" due to impotence in this poet's "The Disappointment." Though first credited to the Earl of Rochester, this poet republished it alongside homoerotic poems about Amintas in "Poems upon Several Occasions."

bonuses.leadin: In a novel by this author, a woman disguises herself as a prostitute, a maid, a grief-stricken widow, and a masked woman called Incognita in order to trick Beauplaisir (“bow-PLAY-seer”). For 10 points each:
parts: Name this writer of amatory fiction such as Fantomina. This author created the rake D’Elmont in the 1719 novel Love in Excess.
Eliza Haywood, Delarivier Manley, and this author were known as “the fair triumvirate of wit.” This author wrote about Hellena dressing as a man to woo Willmore during Carnival in her play The Rover.
In 1741, parodies of Samuel Richardson’s novel Pamela were written by Haywood and this other early English novelist who wrote Tom Jones.
answers: Henry Fielding (Haywood’s parody was The Anti-Pamela, and Fielding’s was Shamela.)

packet.name: L_ Northwestern B, NYU B, Minnesota B, Florida A, Lambert

answers_sanitized: Henry Fielding (Haywood's parody was The Anti-Pamela, and Fielding's was Shamela.)

bonuses.leadin: Susanna Centilivre used this event as the backdrop for her play about the unfaithful Count Bassino, The Perjur’d Husband. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this event that provides the setting for a play that originated the line “there is no sinner like a young saint.” In that play set during this event, a wealthy man forces Belville to take his place in a duel over an expensive courtesan.
The latter play, The Rover, is by this female Restoration author of Oroonoko, whose pseudonym Astrea was later borrowed by Centilivre.

Colonel Fainwell dons four disguises to dupe a woman’s four guardians in a Centilivre play titled “A Bold Stroke” for one of these people. A play titled for this type of person includes a scene in which a man feigning impotence talks with two women about porcelain in sexual double entendres.
answers: wife [accept A Bold Stroke for a Wife or The Country Wife] (The scene is the “china scene” from The Country Wife.)
answers_sanitized: Carnival [accept the Venetian Carnival or the Naples Carnival]
wife [accept A Bold Stroke for a Wife or The Country Wife] (The scene is the "china scene" from The Country Wife.)

bonuses.leadin: This person is "Clad in the beauty of a thousand stars" in a speech which asks her to "make me immortal with a kiss." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this person who is called "the face that launch'd a thousand ships" in Christopher Marlowe's play Doctor Faustus.

Paris's abandonment of his lover for Helen of Troy is depicted in Ovid's Heroides, which was paraphrased by this author in Oenone (ee-NOH-nee) to Paris. Angellica Bianca nearly kills Willmore in this author's play The Rover.

A New York screenwriter juxtaposes Helen with this person in an Anne Carson play titled for this person of Troy. This person is fictionalized as the protagonist's suicidal wife in a play whose set consists of a chair in front of a concentration camp tower.
answers: Helen of Troy [or Helen of Sparta or Helen of Greece]
Marilyn Monroe [or Norma Jeane Baker; accept Norma Jeane Baker of Troy] (the second play is Arthur Miller's After the Fall)

parts: Name this author of The Emperor of the Moon. A play by this author, which was based on Thomas Killigrew's Thomaso, depicts the amorous adventures of Florinda and Hellena at Carnival.
Aphra Behn was employed as a spy by Charles II during this period of English history. William Wycherly and William Congreve were other authors from this period that followed the Interregnum.
This other Restoration author wrote about the Great Fire of London in his poem Annus Mirabilis. This poet also satirized the “true-blue Protestant Poet” Thomas Shadwell in the mock epic Mac Flecknoe.
answers: Aphra Behn (The second play is The Rover.)
English Restoration [or Stuart Restoration; accept Restoration drama or Restoration comedy]

bonuses.leadin: This author worked in the RAF, which he described in his first short story “A Piece of Cake,” before his time as a British spy. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who worked under William Stephenson as a spy in Washington, D. C. His numerous sexual liaisons there possibly inspired his work My Uncle Oswald.

This author used the codenames “Agent 160” and “Astrea” to go on a spying mission to Belgium under King Charles II, as well as a possible trip to Suriname, where a slave uprising may have inspired her novel Oroonoko.
This author of The Power and The Glory drew on his experience as a spy for MI6 to write Our Man in Havana.

bonuses.leadin: In this play, a famous Spanish courtesan named Angellica charges a thousand crowns a month for her services, but Willmore, the title character, somehow manages to charm her into forgoing the fee. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 1677 play, a revision of Thomas Killigrew’s Thomaso, set in Naples at carnival-time. It features Blunt being duped out of all of his belongings, Florinda’s courtship with Don Pedro, and Hellena’s rejection of the convent.

The Rover was an extremely popular example of Restoration comedy. This other dramatist wrote examples of ‘softer’ Restoration comedy, such as Love for Love, and a play which features a famous ‘proviso scene’ between Mirabell and Millamant.

This term for a stock character in Restoration comedy comes from a word for ‘hellraiser’, and describes a man inclined to immoral behaviour, particularly womanising. This character’s ‘Progress’ is tracked in a series of eight paintings by William Hogarth.

bonuses.leadin: British women were allowed to join this profession starting in the 1660s, under the reign of Charles II. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this profession of Elizabeth Barry and Nell Gwynn, who was also a mistress of Charles II. Later women with this profession included several members of the Kemble family.
Barry and Gwynn may have been in the original cast of this author’s play The Rover. This staunch royalist and spy for Charles II satirized anti-Catholic prejudice in The Feign’d Curtizans.

Tragediennes like Barry helped popularize this dramatic genre, examples of which included Nicholas Rowe’s The Fair Penitent and Thomas Otway’s The Orphan. Also called the “pathetic tragedy,” this genre focused on the sufferings of a tragic female heroine.
answers: actor [accept word forms like acting or actress; accept tragedienne; prompt on less specific answers that discuss the theatre or the stage]
she-tragedy [accept pathetic tragedy until “called the” is read; prompt on tragedy]
answers_sanitized: actor [accept word forms like acting or actress; accept tragedienne; prompt on less specific answers that discuss the theatre or the stage]
she-tragedy [accept pathetic tragedy until "called the" is read; prompt on tragedy]

bonuses.leadin: A book with this name by Siri Hustvedt deals with the artist Harriet Burden who sells her work by hiring men to present it as their own. For 10 points each:
parts: This is also the title of a 1666 utopian work by Margaret Cavendish, in which the protagonist reaches the title “new world” via the North Pole and gets crowned Empress of some bird-men and fish-men.
This author compared Margaret Cavendish’s lack of tutoring and disorganized intellect to a giant cucumber choking roses and carnations in a garden in her work A Room of One’s Own.

Right after a discussion of Cavendish, A Room of One’s Own praises this author of Oroonoko and The Rover, who wrote about girls trying to convince their guardian that their lovers are aliens in The Emperor of the Moon.

bonuses.leadin: Vertuso makes a killing manufacturing dildoes for court women such as Cunticulla and Fuckadilla after King Bolloxinion legalizes same-sex sodomy, in an amazingly rude satire of this monarch’s court. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this king who, in real life, patronized actresses such as his lover Nell Gwyn. Appropriately, the term “restoration comedy” refers to plays written during this Stuart king’s “Restoration” to the throne.

The aforementioned satire, Sodom, or the Quintessence of Debauchery, was written by this licentious nobleman, who had affairs with several actress, including Nell Gwyn and Elizabeth Barry. He critiqued rationalism itself in A Satire Against Reason and Mankind.
In this Restoration comedy, Captain Willmore, a character based on aspects of Charles II and Rochester, chases skirts in Naples until conquered by the equally adventurous Hellena.
answers: John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester [accept either underlined name]

bonuses.leadin: In one play by this author, The Comedy of Bristo, or The Pimp, Alexander betrays his friend Leonardo after he sees Camille’s beauty, but the help of the title character and Pindaro’s timely arrival allow the two to wed. F10PE:
parts: Name this Portuguese poet, who also wrote a tragedy in which Coelho and Pacheco execute the title lover of Pedro, despite that woman winning clemency from Pedro’s father Alphonso IV, The Tragedy of Ines de Castro.

Ferreira based the lecherous Hannibal, who regularly reminds the parasite Montalvam of his exploits in Rhodes and who tries to purchase Camille from Bristo, on Pyrgopolynices, the title braggart solider of this Plautine comedy.

The story of the historical Ines de Castro was also tackled in the novel Agnes of Castro by this member of the “fair triumvirate of wit”, who also wrote the Restoration Comedies The Feigned Courtesans and The Rover.
answers: Miles Gloriosus [do not accept The Braggart Soldier or logical English equivalents]
answers_sanitized: Miles Gloriosus [do not accept The Braggart Soldier or logical English equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: Name these seventeenth-century women of British literature, for 10 points each.
parts: This author of the plays The Comical Hash and A Comedy of the Apocryphal Ladies attached her Observations Upon Experimental Philosophy to her early science-fiction novel The Blazing World.

This member of the "fair triumvirate of wit" wrote about Doctor Baliardo being convinced that his daughter and niece's paramours are aliens in The Emperor of the Moon and about a slave renamed Caesar being dismembered while alive in Oronooko.
This member of the Society of Friendship was known as "Orinda" to the subjects of her "friendship lyrics," and is also known for her Letters to Poliarchus and Upon the Double Murder of King Charles.
